title: Chapter 7: The Gates of Agdash
paragraphs: The journey through the radial wastelands took another four exhausting, soul-sucking days. The supplies of muddy water, found in the backpacks of killed scavengers, had come to an end, and the gray ash was clogging the lungs with every breath, settling on the tongue with the taste of rust. Ravan, despite his fully healed hand thanks to the elf's magic, walked with a dull, leaden gaze. This alien, dead world, full of illogical magic and ugly monsters, was slowly, drop by drop, sucking out his will. He looked at his calloused, huge palms, once tenderly holding his son, and realized that he would give anything, all this newfound friendship and salvation, just to return to that cramped but so familiar bathroom at home.

On the fifth day, the horizon shuddered and changed. Through the eternal leaden haze, titanic walls emerged, as if carved from a solid piece of the night sky. This was Agdash—the great city of fire elves, towers above the rotting world like the last lighthouse. Unlike the ruined Observatory, here a feverish, desperate life was in full swing.

Approaching the massive Gates, forged from heavy black metal and decorated with unrecognized runes, the group was relieved to find that the guard was not hostile. Tall elves in shining red-gold armor did not aim their bows at them. After the apocalypse, racial prejudices had faded; King Agsham's elves accepted everyone—humans, dwarves, transparent gidroli. All they needed was for them to be ready to stand on the wall. But the laws of survival remained cruel.

"Welcome to the Gates of Life, travelers," the captain of the guard said, nodding amiably but tiredly. There was no arrogance in his eyes, only bottomless exhaustion from endless shifts. "Agdash is open to all who seek peace. But King Agsham's laws are uniform: to enter and use the protection of the walls, everyone must contribute to the common pot. Food, clean water, or alchemical potions. In this world, nothing comes for free."
Alexander grew grim. They had nothing but rags of torn clothes, an old crossbow without a string, and a couple of kitchen knives.

"We have empty hands, captain," Alexander began diplomatically but with an inner core, stepping forward and pushing his friends behind his back. "We have gone through hell. Let us in, and we will work off every drop of water. My people know how to labor and are ready to build."
The captain shook his head with regret: "I sympathize, human. But the city is overcrowded with refugees. Without payment, I cannot let you in. Such is the regulation."
Yuri, hearing the painfully familiar word "regulation," jerked nervously, clenching his fists, but remained silent.

At that moment, Avotu stepped forward. He had kept in Alexander's shadow until then, hiding his flame under the low-pulled hood of his dusty cloak. The elf threw off the hood. Living, oily-black flame shot up, greedily reflecting in the polished armor of the guards.

The guards gasped in unison. The hubbub at the gates instantly died down. Some instinctively stepped back, their hands reflexively resting on the hilts of their swords. They recognized him. Even after two years of terror, the legends of Sivarus and his last disciple still lived in their bleeding hearts.

"I am Avotu," his deep, vibrating voice boomed over the gates, making even the wasteland wind freeze. "These people are under my personal protection. They are my responsibility. As payment for their passage, I give this."

From the folds of his cloak, he pulled a heavy glass flask, filled to the brim with a shining, thick and hot liquid. It was the blood of the high demon Zakhan he had killed—the rarest, priceless alchemical ingredient, capable of serving as a powerful source of energy for the protective barriers of an entire quarter.
The captain of the guard, with reverence, almost with sacred awe, accepted the flask, bowing his head low:
"It is a great honor for us to receive you, Master Avotu. The Gates of Agdash are open."

The heavy leaves parted with a deafening grinding of gears. The strangers stepped inside, and their breath caught. Agdash was incredible—a paradoxical mixture of grandeur and poverty. Multi-level terraces lit by dim magical lanterns, noisy markets smelling of sweat, where they desperately bargained for a piece of meat from local creatures and crystal chips.

But most of all, Zhenya's attention was caught by strange, clanking metal carts moving without draft animals through the cobbled streets. A pungent smell of hot oil and tingling machine ozone emanated from them.
"What kind of engineering miracle is this?" the worker from his former life exhaled enthusiastically, not believing his own eyes. The gears in his brain immediately spun.
Avotu, whose face remained an inscrutable mask, followed his burning gaze.

"The creations of the Mechanics' Guild," the elf explained dryly. "They construct primitive mechanisms that use residual mania from portal crystals. Useless in a serious fight, but they save the internal logistics of the city."

Zhenya's hands began to itch. For the first time since staying in this nightmare, a meaningful, calm brilliance appeared in his eyes. He realized where his place was. Here, among the soot-stained walls, he was no longer a burden. Here he could create things superior to primitive elven pneumatics.
"Where now? We need to find lodging," Alexander asked, anxiously noting his comrades' inflamed eyes.

"You will stay in the Lower Tier. And I'm going to the palace," Avotu cut off grimly, looking up at the gargantuan obsidian spire rising above the city. "King Agsham won't like that I've returned. But he is the only one who knows how this city works."
